title: Undercut/overcut pressure score from race-lap context and stint opportunity windows
summary: F1 strategy sources emphasize that pit timing depends on degradation, warmup, track position, and whether a rival is within striking range.
rationale: F1 strategy sources emphasize that pit timing depends on degradation, warmup, track position, and whether a rival is within striking range. Current best features are mostly self-state tyre-window heuristics; they appear to underuse competitor-triggered stop pressure.
implementation_hint: Build leakage-safe same-lap context features within each race: percentile rank of `LapTime_Delta`, rank-based local pressure from cars in nearby positions, count of nearby drivers on older/newer compounds, and an `undercut_opportunity` score combining high degradation, hard-to-overtake races, and inferred close-rival pressure. Approximate warmup-sensitive overcut regimes using race-level historical medians of post-stop pace response by compound and year.
expected_effect: Should improve recall on reactive stops caused by nearby rivals rather than pure tyre exhaustion.
risk: No explicit gap-to-car-ahead/behind is available, so proxy design may add noise if positional neighborhoods are weak stand-ins for actual time gaps.
